summary: Riemannian optimization (Absil-Mahony-Sepulchre 2008, Amari 1998, Ganea-Becigneul-Hofmann 2018) is a real and rich literature, but it solves a problem plumbline does NOT have: optimizing smooth functions over CONTINUOUS curved parameter spaces (Stiefel/Grassmann manifolds, hyperbolic space, SPD matrices, statistical manifolds). Smart-contract invariants live in a DISCRETE, symbolic space (boolean predicates with quantifiers, arithmetic operators) with no native Riemannian metric, no tangent space, no exponential map. There is no "curved manifold of invariants" to do gradient descent on in any literal sense. However, JH's underlying intuition - iteratively refine a hypothesis using critic/validator feedback as a directional signal - has a faithful technical realization that is NOT Riemannian: it is TextGrad (Yuksekgonul et al., Nature 2024) "textual gradients," DSPy MIPRO/Bootstrap, and the long-established CEGIS / PIE / LOOPINVGEN / SmartInv / FLAMES / ConVer lineage. These already implement the loop JH wants - candidate -> verifier counterexample -> refine - and they explicitly frame critic feedback as a discrete "gradient" in semantic space. The Riemannian framing is metaphor here; the iterative-refinement-with-validator-as-gradient framing is real and shovel-ready for Day-5 cutoff.
key_findings: Riemannian gradient descent (Absil-Mahony-Sepulchre, 'Optimization Algorithms on Matrix Manifolds', Princeton 2008): the canonical reference. Core mechanic is iterate { compute Euclidean gradient -> project to tangent space at current point -> move along tangent using retraction back to manifold }. Requires: smooth objective, differentiable manifold structure, tangent spaces, retractions. None of these exist for a space of symbolic invariants like 'totalSupply == sum(balances)' or 'forall i,j: i != j -> balances[i] is independent of balances[j]'. Honest verdict: not directly applicable.

Natural gradient (Amari, 'Natural gradient works efficiently in learning', Neural Computation 1998): preconditions the Euclidean gradient by inverse Fisher information matrix to get the steepest descent in KL-divergence rather than parameter-Euclidean distance. Reparameterization-invariant. This IS real and useful - but only when you have a parametric family of probability distributions. The plumbline analog would require viewing 'invariant proposals' as samples from a parameterized distribution over predicates and computing the Fisher of that distribution w.r.t. its parameters - this is exactly what RLHF / PPO does for LLMs (KL penalty to base model). Already implicitly present in any RL-finetuning loop. No need to reinvent.

Hyperbolic neural networks (Ganea, Becigneul, Hofmann, NeurIPS 2018, arXiv:1805.09112): embeds discrete tree/hierarchical data in the Poincare ball where geodesic distance grows exponentially with radius, matching tree-distance growth. Riemannian Adam (Becigneul-Ganea ICLR 2019) does the optimization. Honest applicability to plumbline: invariants do have a partial order (stronger -> weaker), but conflating that with hyperbolic geometry is decoration, not mechanism. Would not survive an honest 'does this earn its complexity' test.

Trust-region methods on Riemannian manifolds (Absil-Baker-Gallivan 2007) and Riemannian L-BFGS (Huang-Gallivan-Absil, also Godaz et al. arXiv:2108.11019 vector-transport-free): generalize quasi-Newton to manifolds via vector transport. Real technique, real convergence guarantees, but again requires the manifold structure to exist. The Day-5 cost to even define a manifold over the invariant DSL is prohibitive and yields no measured precision/recall lift.

REAL applicable lineage - the actual literature for what JH wants: (a) CEGIS / counterexample-guided synthesis (Solar-Lezama 2006-onward) is exactly 'propose invariant -> verifier returns counterexample -> refine in the direction the CE points'. (b) PIE / LOOPINVGEN (Padhi et al. PLDI 2016, arXiv:1707.02029) iteratively learns predicate features from counterexamples. (c) SmartInv (arXiv:2411.09217), FLAMES (arXiv:2510.21401), PropertyGPT, ConVer (arXiv:2605.27051), Quokka (arXiv:2509.21629) are all LLM-driven iterative invariant refinement loops for smart contracts and verification - the same problem plumbline is solving. (d) TextGrad (Yuksekgonul et al., Nature 2024, arXiv:2406.07496) is the most direct analog to JH's framing: it treats critic LLM feedback as a 'textual gradient' that backpropagates through a computation graph of prompts/outputs to update upstream components. DSPy MIPRO (Khattab et al.) is the gradient-free optimizer equivalent.

Honest verdict on the 'manifold of invariants' framing: it is METAPHOR, not technique. There is no smooth structure on the space of symbolic invariants that makes Riemannian machinery earn its keep over (a) direct critic-feedback refinement loops, (b) RL-style update with KL regularization, or (c) discrete tree-search / genetic mutation over the predicate AST. Two recent papers do connect Riemannian geometry to discrete computation (arXiv:2512.00196 'Emergent Riemannian geometry over learning discrete computations', and the 'Neural Solving Uninterpreted Predicates with Abstract Gradient Descent' work ACM TOSEM 2024) - but these RELAX discrete predicates into continuous neural representations first, then optimize there. That relaxation itself is the unsolved research problem and would consume months, not days.

What IS measurable and Day-5-feasible: the v2 multi-mode invariant menu (scalar/relational/temporal/counting) plus a critic loop that scores 'vacuousness' (how often halmos refutes / trivially discharges) is a legitimate textual-gradient implementation. Score the candidate, ask the critic 'this invariant scored X because Y - propose a strengthening/relaxation that addresses Y', iterate up to a budget cap. This is TextGrad-flavored without needing the TextGrad library. Total implementation cost: 1-2 days of prompt+harness work, fits in $25 OpenRouter budget if iteration cap is ~5-8 per candidate.

The dangerous failure mode to avoid: building a Riemannian-themed wrapper (computing 'tangent directions' over an arbitrary embedding of invariant strings, doing 'exponential maps' that are really just edit operations) would be Potemkin geometry. It would not produce measurably better CLEAN scores than the same edit operations called by their honest names. The user's own CLAUDE.md is explicit on this: 'is what I'm doing theater? a potemkin village? fake? ...is it.. fucking.. real?' Naming an edit operation an 'exponential map' is theater.
concrete_examples:
Riemannian gradient descent skeleton (Absil-Mahony-Sepulchre style):
for k in range(max_iter):
    grad_eucl = euclidean_gradient(f, x_k)
    grad_riem = project_to_tangent(grad_eucl, x_k)   # remove normal component
    eta_k = line_search(...)
    x_{k+1} = retraction(x_k, -eta_k * grad_riem)    # back onto manifold
The required ingredients (project_to_tangent, retraction) have NO analog for symbolic predicate spaces without inventing a relaxation first.

Natural gradient update (Amari 1998):
theta_{k+1} = theta_k - eta * F(theta_k)^{-1} * grad L(theta_k)
where F = E[grad log p(x|theta) grad log p(x|theta)^T] is the Fisher information matrix.
The plumbline echo of this already exists implicitly: KL-penalty in any RLHF setup is exactly natural-gradient-flavored regularization toward a base distribution.

TextGrad-style loop for invariant refinement (faithful to JH's framing, NO Riemannian machinery needed):
inv = initial_proposal(contract)
for step in range(max_steps):
    score = vacuousness_score(inv, halmos_result)
    if score < threshold and halmos_discharges(inv):
        return inv
    feedback = critic_llm(inv, halmos_counterexample, score)   # the 'textual gradient'
    inv = refiner_llm(inv, feedback)                            # the 'update step'
This is the legitimate version of 'gradient descent through invariant space'.

Honest reframing for plumbline v2: do NOT call this 'Riemannian manifold navigation.' Call it 'critic-guided iterative refinement with multi-mode invariant menu' or just 'CEGIS with LLM proposer.' Same algorithm, no overclaim, survives an adversarial reviewer. The technical content is in (a) the multi-mode menu earning CLEAN lift, (b) the vacuousness score being a real measurable signal, (c) the critic feedback actually steering refinements toward halmos-dischargeable invariants. None of that needs differential geometry.
plumbline_recommendation: For Day-5 cutoff (Fri 2026-06-13): do NOT implement Riemannian machinery. It is a category error - the invariant space is discrete and symbolic, not a smooth manifold, and any attempt to force a metric/tangent/retraction onto it within 4 days will be Potemkin geometry that produces no measurable CLEAN-score lift. Instead, implement the honest version of JH's underlying intuition: a TextGrad-style critic loop (1-2 days of work, fits in $25 budget) on top of the v2 multi-mode menu. Concretely: (1) define a vacuousness_score from halmos outcomes (refuted-trivially / refuted-meaningfully / discharged / timeout); (2) on every non-discharged candidate, feed (candidate, halmos verdict, score, counterexample if any) to a critic LLM that returns structured textual feedback naming WHICH mode (scalar/relational/temporal/counting) the candidate is missing; (3) refiner LLM produces next candidate guided by that feedback; (4) cap iterations per contract at 5-8 to stay in budget; (5) measure CLEAN lift vs v2-without-loop as the ground-truth A/B. Cite TextGrad (Yuksekgonul et al. Nature 2024), CEGIS, PIE, SmartInv if framing for a paper later - those are the real lineage. Save the Riemannian literature for a future post-cutoff exploration ONLY if you first build a continuous neural relaxation of the invariant DSL, which is a months-long research project, not a Day-5 feature.
sources: https://dl.acm.org/doi/full/10.1145/3675394 (Neural Solving Uninterpreted Predicates with Abstract Gradient Descent, ACM TOSEM 2024)
https://arxiv.org/pdf/2512.00196 (Emergent Riemannian geometry over learning discrete computations on continuous manifolds)

summary: Manifold learning on code is a real and active subfield, with three lineages directly relevant to plumbline: (1) hyperbolic embeddings of ASTs/call-graphs (Nickel-Kiela Poincaré applied to code by Hou et al. 2019, recent "Natural Geometry of Code" 2024), which exploit the tree-shape of programs — but every paper is classification/clone-detection, not invariant inference; (2) latent-program VAEs (Syntax-Directed VAE, Optimus, LPN 2024) which produce a continuous space where interpolation yields valid programs and gradient ascent at test time refines candidates against a likelihood signal — this is the closest published analogue to the "magical path-finding via critic gradient" framing JH wants; (3) neural loop-invariant inference (CODE2INV NeurIPS'18, CLN2INV ICLR'20, G-CLN PLDI'20) which encodes invariants in a continuous logic-network latent and uses verifier counterexamples as a CEGIS-style update signal — this IS literally a latent-space-navigation-for-invariants pipeline, but their "latent" is a learned-from-scratch differentiable formula representation, not a frozen sentence-embedding manifold. Critically, NO published work applies UMAP/Isomap/diffusion-maps to a corpus of natural-language finding embeddings to navigate toward verifier-discharging invariants — the gap plumbline could land in is real but unproven. For Day-5: the honest answer is that learning the intrinsic manifold (TwoNN dimension estimate, UMAP/Isomap of the 1240 bge-small-en findings) is a 1-hour script and could plausibly *retrieve* better seed invariants for v2's multi-mode extraction; but actually *navigating* the manifold as a Pass-A refinement loop (gradient on critic feedback through a frozen sentence encoder) is research-grade and probably a follow-up paper, not a Day-5 deliverable.
key_findings: CODE2INV (Si et al., NeurIPS 2018) and CLN2INV (Ryan et al., ICLR 2020) are the DIRECT precedent: they encode loop invariants in a learned continuous latent space and use SMT-verifier counterexamples to drive gradient updates — this is exactly the 'critic feedback as gradient' loop JH described. CLN2INV solved all 124 problems in the Code2Inv benchmark and is the state-of-the-art baseline plumbline should be aware of. Repo: github.com/PL-ML/CLN2INV.

Latent Program Networks (Bonnet & Macfarlane, 2024, arXiv:2411.08706) is the cleanest published instance of 'gradient ascent through a VAE-style program latent at test time': encoder maps I/O pairs to z, then ∇z Σ log p(y|x,z) refines z, then decoder generates. 256-dim latent for ARC-AGI, 32-dim for simpler tasks, 0-5 gradient steps. This is the architecture template for the 'iterative navigation' framing — but it's program-synthesis, not invariant-inference, and requires training the encoder/decoder.

Hyperbolic embeddings of code are real: Hou et al. 2019 'Hyperbolic Function Embedding' (Symmetry 11(2):254) and 'The Natural Geometry of Code' (HypeCodeNet, OpenReview 2024) demonstrate that Ricci curvature of function-call graphs is negative, so Poincaré-ball embeddings give lower distortion than Euclidean. BUT every application is downstream classification (clone detection, code completion, link prediction) — never invariant generation. The Poincaré-ball geometry is for tree-like hierarchical data and may NOT match the structure of plumbline's NL-finding corpus.

SmartInv (Wang et al., S&P 2024, arXiv:2411.09217) is the closest existing work to plumbline's pipeline: multimodal (source + NL) finetuning for smart-contract invariant inference. It does NOT use manifold learning — it uses tiered prompting + cross-entropy loss on labeled ground-truth invariants across 10 transactional contexts. This is the published competitor; plumbline's manifold-navigation framing would be a genuinely novel angle vs SmartInv if executed.

Intrinsic-dimension estimation is a 1-hour problem and would tell JH whether the manifold framing is even meaningful for his corpus. TwoNN estimator (Facco et al.; intRinsic R package, arXiv:2102.11425; scikit-dimension Python) needs only the two nearest-neighbor distances per point. For 1240 points in 384-D, expect intrinsic dimension somewhere in 10-30 if findings are semantically clustered. 'Less is More: Local Intrinsic Dimensions of Contextual Language Models' (2025, arXiv:2506.01034) shows LIDs vary by 5x across regions of transformer embedding space — meaningful structure to navigate IF it's there.

Practical libraries are mature: sklearn.manifold (Isomap, SpectralEmbedding/diffusion-maps-equivalent, TSNE) is already installed in plumbline's env; umap-learn (`pip install umap-learn`) gives UMAP + parametric UMAP with sklearn API; geomstats gives proper Riemannian operations (Poincaré ball, SPD, hypersphere) with NumPy/PyTorch/TF backends and works at GPU scale; scikit-dimension for intrinsic-dim estimators (TwoNN, MLE, ABID). geomstats is overkill for Day-5 — sklearn.manifold + umap-learn covers it.

Latent-space arithmetic on code/program embeddings is published (code2vec captures semantic analogies, Optimus shows sentence-vector arithmetic, Syntax-Directed VAE shows valid-program interpolation — Dai et al. ICLR 2018, arXiv:1802.08786) but NO paper uses arithmetic-style operations (finding_A − finding_B + target_context) to STEER toward verifier-discharging invariants. This is genuinely open territory and would be a real research contribution if it works.

Honest gap: every cited pipeline that navigates latent space via gradient (LPN, CLN2INV, CODE2INV, gradient-based program repair arXiv:2505.17703) trains its own differentiable decoder. Plumbline's 1240 findings live in a FROZEN sentence-encoder space (bge-small-en-v1.5) — there is no decoder back to invariant-text, so 'gradient ascent on critic signal' has no obvious differentiable path. The realistic Day-5 move is RETRIEVAL/NEIGHBORHOOD-SEARCH on the manifold (cluster, k-NN, geodesic walks), not gradient navigation.
concrete_examples:
TwoNN intrinsic-dimension estimate on plumbline's 1240×384 findings (run-in-1-hour, validates manifold framing):
```python
import pickle, numpy as np
from sklearn.neighbors import NearestNeighbors
with open('tools/findings_index.pkl','rb') as f:
    idx = pickle.load(f)
X = np.stack([e['embedding'] for e in idx])  # (1240, 384)
nbrs = NearestNeighbors(n_neighbors=3).fit(X)
d,_ = nbrs.kneighbors(X)
mu = d[:,2]/d[:,1]                # ratio r2/r1
id_hat = X.shape[0]/np.log(mu).sum()  # TwoNN MLE for intrinsic dim
print('intrinsic dim =', id_hat)
```
If id_hat ~10-30 → meaningful manifold to navigate. If id_hat ~150+ → embedding noise dominates, manifold framing is wishful.

UMAP + HDBSCAN for finding-cluster retrieval (BERTopic recipe — Day-5 tractable, real lift expected):
```python
import umap, hdbscan
reducer = umap.UMAP(n_components=15, metric='cosine', min_dist=0.0, n_neighbors=15)
Z = reducer.fit_transform(X)            # (1240, 15)
clu = hdbscan.HDBSCAN(min_cluster_size=8).fit(Z)
# For a target bug embedding q (Sherlock 1259 DRE):
q = embedder.embed(['Reentrancy on collateral withdrawal …'])
qZ = reducer.transform(q)
from sklearn.neighbors import NearestNeighbors
nbrs = NearestNeighbors(n_neighbors=20).fit(Z)
_, neigh = nbrs.kneighbors(qZ)
seed_findings = [idx[i] for i in neigh[0]]
# Pass A v2 reads multi-mode invariants from these 20 nearest findings → tighter prior than random sampling.
```

CLN2INV-style critic-loop sketch (NOT Day-5 tractable; this is the follow-up paper):
```
for iter in range(K):
    z_t = current_invariant_embedding
    formula_t = decoder(z_t)            # ← plumbline has NO decoder yet
    result = halmos.check(formula_t)
    if result.PASS: return formula_t
    cex = result.counterexample
    # CLN2INV move: differentiable t-norm scores formula on cex,
    # gradient pushes z away from cex-satisfying region.
    z_{t+1} = z_t - lr * grad_z(loss(formula_t, cex))
```
The blocker is decoder(z) → formula text. Options: (a) LLM-conditioned-on-z (z as prompt prefix, not differentiable end-to-end — semi-grad only); (b) train a small grammar-VAE decoder on the 1240 findings (Day-5 impossible from scratch); (c) skip the decoder, use z as RAG-retrieval key into formula templates (this collapses to the UMAP-retrieval recipe above and is the honest Day-5 path).

Hyperbolic-embed plumbline findings via geomstats (worth a probe if intrinsic-dim looks tree-shaped):
```python
from geomstats.geometry.poincare_ball import PoincareBall
from geomstats.learning.frechet_mean import FrechetMean
ball = PoincareBall(dim=10)
# Project bge embeddings via tangent-space exp_map; geodesic distance respects hierarchy.
# Worth ~1 hour to test, but only justified if δ-hyperbolicity of the k-NN graph is low.
```
Compute δ-hyperbolicity first (NetworkX graph from k-NN of cosine sims, then Gromov-product 4-point inequality). If δ ≤ 1 → Poincaré meaningful; if δ ≫ 1 → Euclidean/UMAP wins.
plumbline_recommendation:
DO the manifold-RETRIEVAL angle by Day-5. SKIP the manifold-NAVIGATION (gradient) angle as a follow-up paper.

Concrete Day-5 plan (~3 hours total, ~$0 API cost):

1. (30 min) Run TwoNN intrinsic-dim estimator on the 1240×384 findings matrix. Decision gate: if id_hat ∈ [8, 40] → proceed. If id_hat > 100 → the 'manifold' framing is mostly noise and you should NOT pitch it; go back to plain k-NN cosine retrieval (which is already a sub-case of manifold methods).

2. (1 hr) Add `tools/manifold_retrieve.py`: fit UMAP(n_components=15, cosine, min_dist=0) on the corpus once, save reducer + reduced matrix. For any target bug description (e.g., Sherlock 1259 DRE), transform query, k-NN-retrieve top-20 nearest findings in UMAP space. This becomes the v2 multi-mode seed retriever. Expected lift over flat cosine k-NN: 5-15% on CLEAN, because UMAP global-structure preservation + HDBSCAN clusters give context-coherent neighborhoods rather than nearest-individual-point matches. Cheap, real, measurable.

3. (1 hr) Wire `structural_proposer` to consume the UMAP-retrieved seeds for the Sherlock 1259 fire-and-measure on Friday. Run the measurement; report CLEAN delta vs v2-baseline (no UMAP retrieval).

4. (30 min) Honest writeup: this is RETRIEVAL on the manifold, NOT navigation via gradient. The CLN2INV/LPN-style gradient-critic loop requires a differentiable decoder back to formula text, which plumbline does not have and cannot build by Friday. Frame it as 'manifold-aware retrieval prior for multi-mode extraction', citing CODE2INV/CLN2INV/LPN as the gradient-navigation lineage that the follow-up paper would extend toward.

Do NOT pitch this as 'iterative gradient navigation through invariant manifold' yet — you don't have a decoder, you don't have a differentiable critic→invariant path, and over-claiming will burn the honest signal. The retrieval version is real, measurable, and demonstrably grounded in the manifold literature. The navigation version is the v3/paper move once you have a grammar-VAE or LoRA-tuned decoder.
sources: https://www.cis.upenn.edu/~mhnaik/papers/neurips18.pdf (CODE2INV — Si et al. NeurIPS 2018, neural loop-invariant inference with CEGIS-style counter-example loop)

summary: Iterative refinement of candidate invariants via counterexample feedback is a 25-year-old, well-formalized program-verification idea (CEGAR 2000; Houdini 2001; IC3/PDR 2011; ICE 2014; CHC/Spacer; Liquid Types), and there is now a substantial 2023-2025 body of LLM-proposer + SMT-critic work (Lemur, Clause2Inv, Quokka, LEMUR-style frameworks for Dafny/ESBMC) that reuses the CEGAR shape with LLMs in the proposer slot. JH's "extract -> validator refutes -> re-extract with reason" loop IS structurally CEGAR/Lemur-shaped — not metaphor. The HONEST caveat: LLM-in-the-loop refinement empirically PLATEAUS at iteration 2-3 (FeedbackEval, Debugging Decay Index, Self-Refine self-bias studies); without an EXTERNAL oracle the loop drifts (self-bias monotonically amplifies). plumbline already has the right external oracle (halmos/the adversarial validator), which is exactly the precondition that makes the loop converge in the literature. The right operational form for the Day-5 cutoff is a 3-iteration cap with a stop-on-accept and a stop-on-no-change criterion, costing ~3-5x the single-shot API budget. Convergence guarantees are absent in general (CEGAR is sound but not complete on undecidable problems); the realistic expected lift, by analogy to FeedbackEval / Lemur (107/133 with avg 4.7 attempts), is moving from 14% CLEAN to ~40-60% CLEAN if validator counterexamples are concrete enough to point at a fixable feature.
key_findings: CEGAR (Clarke/Grumberg/Jha/Lu/Veith CAV 2000) is the canonical loop: abstract -> model-check -> if spurious counterexample, refine abstraction with it; if real counterexample, return bug. Termination is NOT guaranteed in general (undecidable verification), but the loop is SOUND — every produced invariant is correct, every produced counterexample is real. JH's loop has the same shape with the LLM as the abstractor.

Houdini (Flanagan/Leino FME 2001) is the closest match operationally: start with a LARGE set of candidate invariants, iteratively DROP those the verifier can't prove, until fixpoint. Convergence is GUARANTEED in polynomial SAT calls because the candidate set is monotonically shrinking. Key insight for plumbline: monotone drop (not regenerate) gives convergence; LLM regenerate-from-scratch does not.

ICE-learning (Garg/Loding/Madhusudan/Neider CAV 2014, extending Sharma/Aiken POPL 2014) formalizes three counterexample types: positive (must be in inv), negative (must not), and IMPLICATION (if x in inv then y must be in inv). ICE is provably more robust than positive/negative-only learning. plumbline's validator currently produces refute-reasons; mapping those into implication form (a counterexample x where inv(x) holds but x is not a real invariant) gives ICE-shape and is what the literature shows actually converges.

Lemur (ICLR 2024) is the closest published analog to JH's plan: LLM proposes invariants -> ESBMC/Z3 verifier checks -> on failure, repair oracle is invoked with the failed property as context. Solves 107/133 Code2Inv benchmarks at avg 4.7 attempts; on SV-COMP 26/50 at avg 9.1, with 16/26 needing >6 proposals. Termination is bounded by k=attempts-per-goal parameter, NOT by convergence. This is the realistic shape and cost envelope to plan for.

LLM iterative refinement empirically PLATEAUS at iteration 2-3 across multiple 2024-2025 studies (FeedbackEval: gains plateau by repair@3; Debugging Decay Index: 60-80% of debugging capability lost within 2-3 attempts; Self-Refine self-bias study: monotonic amplification of self-bias without external feedback). The loop converges when there is an EXTERNAL oracle (compiler, SMT solver, halmos), and oscillates/degrades when the critic is another LLM with no ground truth.

Self-bias is the failure mode JH should specifically expect IF the validator is also LLM-based without symbolic grounding: 'LLMs systematically overrate their own generations during in-context critique/correction'. plumbline's halmos-as-oracle path is exactly the design that the empirical literature says works — but Pass A's adversarial validator must surface CONCRETE counterexamples (a witnessing state), not LLM-generated 'this looks wrong' critique, or the loop will accept-without-improvement.

DAIKON's dynamic+static hybrid (Ernst et al.; ESC/Java integration) is a different point in the design space: generate MANY likely invariants from execution traces, then filter with a static checker. This is the 'cheap propose, expensive verify' shape. For plumbline this maps onto generating a batch of candidate extractions per contract and Pareto-filtering rather than serially refining one — operationally cheaper if API budget is the constraint.

CHC/Spacer (Komuravelli/Gurfinkel/Chaki) generalizes IC3/PDR to Constrained Horn Clauses and is the modern backbone of program verifiers (SeaHorn, JayHorn). The refinement signal is INDUCTIVE GENERALIZATION from a failed query — block the bad state, then strengthen the block with an inductive generalization. plumbline's validator can be made to do the same: when refuting, output not just the failure but a GENERALIZED reason ('all extractions of this shape fail'), which is far stronger feedback than a single counterexample.
concrete_examples:
Lemur core loop (paraphrased from the paper): ```
while attempts < k:
  cand = O_propose(goal, context, prior_failures)
  status = verifier.check(cand => goal)
  if status == proved: return cand
  if status == unknown: cand = O_repair(cand, status); continue
  if status == refuted_with_cex: context += format_cex(cex); continue
return FAIL
``` Theorem 4.1: 'Alg. 1 terminates with success/fail/Unknown' — bounded termination, NOT convergence to truth.

CEGAR loop (Clarke et al. CAV 2000, abstracted): ```
A = initial_abstraction(P)
loop:
  result = model_check(A, phi)
  if result == valid: return PROVED
  cex = result.counterexample
  if is_real(cex, P): return BUG(cex)
  A = refine(A, cex)   # eliminate this spurious cex
``` Soundness: every PROVED is correct, every BUG is a real bug. Completeness: NOT guaranteed; loop may run forever on undecidable instances.

Houdini loop (Flanagan/Leino, paraphrased): ```
C = generate_candidate_invariants(P)  # large set, possibly false
while True:
  bad = {c in C : not verifier.prove(P, C \ {c} => c)}
  if bad is empty: return C  # fixpoint, all are inductive
  C = C \ bad
``` Convergence: polynomial in |C| SAT calls because C shrinks monotonically. plumbline analog: have Pass A propose 5-10 candidate extractions in one shot, then validator-PRUNE rather than refine-one.

ICE-style counterexample format that converges (Garg et al. CAV 2014, adapted to plumbline): instead of 'validator refuted: <text>', return structured ```{"witness_state": s, "why_not_invariant": "violates X under tx Y", "shape_hint": "missing a counting clause over balance"}``` so the LLM can route the fix to the right MODE of v2's multi-mode redesign. Without the shape_hint, refinement is mode-blind and the LLM will likely re-propose in the wrong mode.
plumbline_recommendation: DO THE LOOP — but in the Houdini/Lemur shape, not the naive self-refine shape. Concretely, for the Day-5 Sherlock 1259 DRE fire-and-measure: (1) Cap iterations at k=3 (literature is unanimous that plateau hits by iter 3 and Lemur's median is similar). (2) Add a no-change stop: if the validator's refute_reason is identical to a previous refute_reason, halt and return the last candidate marked 'unrefined' — this prevents the self-bias oscillation that destroys closed-loop LLM systems. (3) Format the validator counterexample as STRUCTURED (witness + reason + mode-hint), not free-text, so v2's multi-mode router can act on it. (4) As an inexpensive parallel arm, also run a Houdini-style batch propose-and-prune: ask Pass A for 5 candidates in one call, validator-filter, keep survivors — this is cheaper per problem ($0.03 vs $0.10) and converges in 1 round by construction. Honest expected lift: based on FeedbackEval/Lemur numbers, expect Pass A v2 + 3-iter refinement to lift CLEAN from 14% to roughly 35-55% on Sherlock 1259, NOT to 90%+ — refinement helps when the v2 modes are expressive enough that some valid invariant is REACHABLE from the proposal manifold; it cannot rescue contracts where the true invariant is outside v2's mode-space. Budget at $25 / ~3000 tokens per refine call / 3 iters = ~150 contracts of headroom, which covers DRE comfortably. Build the prototype; don't theorize further.

summary: RL applied to theorem proving is a real, mature field (HOList 2019, GPT-f 2020, TacticZero 2021, HyperTree 2022, AlphaProof 2024/Nature 2025), and AlphaZero-style MCTS over proof states is the dominant paradigm. RL applied to *invariant inference* specifically has a smaller but pointed literature — Code2Inv (NeurIPS 2018) is exactly the RL-policy-over-invariant-grammar formulation JH is describing, and CLN2INV improved on it dramatically (1.1s/problem, all 124 Code2Inv problems solved). For 2023+, the field has largely moved past pure-RL on this specific task to LLM+SMT iterative-refinement loops (Lemur ICLR 2024, PropertyGPT NDSS 2024, and 2025 work hitting 100% on Code2Inv with 1-2 proposals/instance in ~14-55s). That LLM+counterexample-feedback pattern IS what JH calls "magical path-finding in the manifold" — it's a 1-step lookahead policy without MCTS rollouts, and it dominates the recent SOTA. For a Day-5 cutoff with $25 API budget, MCTS over invariant search space is not feasible: training even a tiny value/policy net needs many proof attempts per state, and JH has no labelled training corpus. But an iterative critic-feedback loop (Lemur-style, no RL training) is feasible in hours and is the strict generalization of plumbline's 1-shot extract→discharge — and it's the proven SOTA pattern.
key_findings: AlphaProof (DeepMind, Nature 2025, doi:10.1038/s41586-025-09833-y) couples a pretrained LM with AlphaZero-style RL inside Lean 4; trained against tens of millions of auto-formalized problems, hit IMO 2024 silver-medal (P1/P2/P6). The training cost was enormous (DeepMind-scale TPU). This is the high-water mark, not a template for a solo researcher on a 5-day cutoff.

TacticZero (Wu et al., NeurIPS 2021, arxiv:2102.09756) is the cleanest 'RL on proof state' paper: MDP where state = derivation paths in HOL4, action = tactic+arguments, reward = proof closes. Trained end-to-end with policy gradient from scratch; beat HOL4 hammers on unseen problems. Required substantial compute and a proof corpus to bootstrap.

HyperTree Proof Search (Lample et al., NeurIPS 2022, arxiv:2205.11491) modified AlphaZero MCTS into hypertree search for proofs; improved miniF2F from 31% to 42% on Lean. Key insight: standard MCTS doesn't map cleanly to proof search because subgoals are conjunctive (AND-nodes), not the OR-nodes MCTS assumes — they had to redesign the tree algorithm. This is a non-trivial engineering hurdle that anyone attempting MCTS-for-invariants would re-hit.

Code2Inv (Si et al., NeurIPS 2018, arxiv:1707.04207 / TACAS 2020) is the direct precedent for what JH proposes: graph neural net over program AST, RL policy (policy gradient, REINFORCE) generates invariant candidates from a BNF grammar, SMT solver checks them, reward = proof closes. Worked but slow and limited. CLN2INV (Ryan et al., ICLR 2020, arxiv:1909.11542) replaced the RL with Continuous Logic Networks (gradient descent on relaxed SMT formulas via execution traces) — solved 124/124 Code2Inv problems in 1.1s/problem average, 40× faster than Code2Inv. The lesson: for invariant inference specifically, RL was NOT the winning architecture — differentiable relaxation of the SMT semantics was.

Lemur (Wu et al., ICLR 2024, arxiv:2310.04870) is the iterative LLM-as-proposer + automated-reasoner-as-checker pattern. Solves 107/133 Code2Inv with GPT-4 + ESBMC in 10-min timeout, typically converges in 4 iterations. No RL training — just prompted GPT-4 with counterexample feedback. This is the architecture JH should actually copy: it's exactly plumbline-but-iterated.

PropertyGPT (Liu et al., NDSS 2024, arxiv:2405.02580) is the smart-contract-specific version: GPT-4 + RAG from human-written property database + iterative compilation-error repair + weighted ranking. Achieved 80% recall on ground-truth properties, found 12 zero-day flaws with bug bounties on Ethereum contracts. The retrieval+iterative-repair pattern (not RL) is the proven recipe in JH's actual domain.

2025 SOTA (arxiv:2508.00419, 'Loop Invariant Generation: A Hybrid Framework') reports 100% on Code2Inv (133/133) using O1/O3-mini + Z3 in tight generate-check loop with only 1-2 proposals per instance, 14-55s wall-clock. The trend is clear: reasoning-model + tight critic loop beats both pure-RL and 1-shot extraction. No training, just prompt + feedback.

Concrete feasibility math for plumbline Day-5: an MCTS rollout policy needs ≥100s of trajectories per state to give meaningful UCB statistics. Each rollout = LLM call (~$0.01 Sonnet 4.5) + halmos discharge (~30s-5min wall-clock for non-trivial invariants). At $25 budget that's ~2500 LLM calls total. A single MCTS run with branching factor 5 and depth 4 with 100 rollouts per node = 500+ calls = $5 per contract. Doable for ~5 contracts but no training data accumulates between runs — so it's not really RL, it's just expensive single-shot tree search. The Lemur/PropertyGPT pattern (linear iteration with critic feedback, depth ~4) uses 4-10 calls/contract = $0.04-0.10/contract. 50-100× cheaper for similar SOTA.
concrete_examples: Code2Inv RL formulation (NeurIPS 2018): state = (program graph encoding, current partial invariant), action = grammar production rule from BNF, reward = +1 if Z3 verifies invariant else 0 (sparse), policy = GNN→tree-LSTM→attention→softmax over actions, training = REINFORCE policy gradient. Failure mode: sparse reward, action space explodes with grammar size.

HyperTree (Lample 2022) modification of AlphaZero MCTS: standard MCTS treats each child as an OR (try alternatives, one success suffices). Proof states have AND-children (subgoal a AND subgoal b BOTH must close). HTPS tracks a 'partial proof hypertree' where a node is solved iff all conjuncts are solved. This is the non-trivial algorithmic adaptation — naive MCTS over proof states underperforms best-first search. JH would need to make the same adaptation if invariants compose conjunctively.

AlphaProof training scale (Nature 2025): ~1M auto-formalized problems from Mathlib + synthetic, RL fine-tuning over weeks of TPU time, plus per-problem test-time search budget measured in CPU-days. The 'reinforcement learning' here is multi-stage: (1) supervised fine-tune on existing proofs, (2) expert iteration with AlphaZero-style policy improvement against the Lean kernel as ground-truth reward.

Pragmatic plumbline architecture suggestion (no RL, just better critic loop):
```
ProposeRound(contract, k=5):
    candidates = LLM.propose(contract, modes=[scalar,relational,temporal,counting])
    discharge_results = parallel_halmos(candidates, timeout=60s)
    refute_feedback = [(c, halmos_counterexample(c)) for c in failures]
    if any(discharge_results == VERIFIED): return success
    return ProposeRound(contract, feedback=refute_feedback, k=k-1)
```
This is Lemur/PropertyGPT applied to plumbline. ~4-10 LLM calls per contract, $0.04-0.10/contract, no training needed.
plumbline_recommendation: DO NOT attempt MCTS or RL within the Day-5 cutoff. The honest assessment: every successful RL-on-verification system in the literature (TacticZero, HyperTree, AlphaProof) required substantial training corpus and compute that plumbline does not have, and even Code2Inv (the closest direct precedent for invariant-RL) was outperformed by non-RL approaches (CLN2INV's differentiable SMT relaxation, then Lemur/PropertyGPT's LLM+critic iteration). INSTEAD, implement the Lemur/PropertyGPT pattern: turn plumbline's 1-shot extract into a 3-5-iteration loop where halmos counterexamples (not just FAIL/PASS but the actual concrete (caller, amount, state) witness) are fed back into the next proposer prompt. This is the empirical SOTA pattern (2024-2025), needs zero training, fits in $25 API budget, and is a strict generalization of v2's multi-mode design. The 'manifold path-finding' metaphor maps onto this perfectly — each refute carves a constraint into the next proposal's context, navigating the LLM's prior toward a discharge-able invariant. If, after Day-5 fire-and-measure shows the iterative loop matters, then a follow-up paper can frame this as 'RL with the LLM as policy and halmos as environment' and accumulate trajectories for actual training. But for the cutoff: critic loop, not MCTS.

summary: LLM-driven tree-search for problem solving is real and well-evidenced, but the cost-vs-payoff math is brutal and well documented. Tree of Thoughts (Yao et al. 2023, NeurIPS) gave Game-of-24 a 4%->74% jump using BFS with breadth b=5, propose-then-value-evaluator, but at roughly $0.74/task and ~100x the tokens of CoT. Graph of Thoughts (Besta et al., AAAI 2024) cuts ~31% of ToT cost with aggregation operators on sorting-like tasks. AlphaProof (DeepMind, Nature 2025) is the closest structural analog to plumbline Pass A v2.5 — LLM proposer + AlphaZero-style MCTS + Lean kernel as hard verifier — but it lives in a many-GPU regime, not a $25 API budget. Critically, SMARTINV (Wang et al., S&P 2024) already shipped "Tier of Thought" for smart-contract invariant inference and reports 3.5x more bug-critical invariants and 4x more bugs vs INVCON/VERISMART — this is direct prior art for the plumbline thesis, not a metaphor. For Day-5, a small (b=3, depth=2) ToT with halmos as terminal evaluator is the right shape; the budget supports it only if Pass A is gated to <=15 candidate contracts. Best-of-N with halmos discharge as the picker is the cheaper and arguably wiser MVP — it captures ~80% of ToT's gain at ~25% of ToT's cost in published comparisons.
key_findings: Tree of Thoughts (Yao et al. 2023, arxiv:2305.10601, NeurIPS 2023): four design knobs: (a) thought decomposition, (b) generator (propose=sequential constrained, sample=i.i.d. open-ended), (c) state evaluator (value=scalar per state, vote=comparative across siblings), (d) search algorithm (BFS with beam b, or DFS with backtrack). Game-of-24 used propose+value+BFS b=5; lifted GPT-4 from 4% (CoT) and 7.3% (IO) to 74%. Cost: ~5.5k completion tokens/task and ~$0.74/task on GPT-4, i.e. ~100x CoT compute.

Graph of Thoughts (Besta et al., arxiv:2308.09687, AAAI 2024): adds aggregate and refine operators on top of ToT, framework tuple (G,T,E,R). Reports +62% sorting quality and >=31% cost reduction vs ToT, but the cost win comes from being able to merge thought-subtrees — which only helps when sub-results are composable. For invariant inference this is questionable: candidate invariants are not easily aggregated, so GoT's structural advantage may not transfer.

Self-consistency (Wang et al. 2022, arxiv:2203.11171) and best-of-N: the cheapest non-trivial tree-search analogs. Sample N candidates, pick by vote (self-consistency) or by external verifier score (best-of-N). 2025 surveys (e.g. arxiv:2501.10069) note best-of-N often beats MCTS at the same token budget. For invariant inference, best-of-N with halmos as the picker is the right adaptation: pick the candidate halmos actually discharges, not the majority candidate.

AlphaProof (DeepMind, Nature 2025): solved IMO 2024 problems 1, 2, 6 at silver-medal level. Architecture = (1) autoformalizer LLM, (2) policy/value network proposes Lean tactics, (3) AlphaZero MCTS over tactic tree, (4) Lean kernel as hard verifier — wrong branches immediately pruned. The Lean kernel as terminal evaluator is structurally identical to halmos in plumbline. Validates the architecture; the system used many GPU-days/problem, not $25, so the structural lesson is real but the scale is not transferable.

LeanDojo + ReProver (Yang et al., arxiv:2306.15626, NeurIPS 2023) + VerMCTS (arxiv:2402.08147): ReProver uses best-first search (not full MCTS) and competes with MCTS systems. VerMCTS uses the verifier as a heuristic to evaluate PARTIAL programs. Two transferable patterns: (a) best-first by critic score is often as good as MCTS at a fraction of the cost; (b) partial-invariant halmos checks can prune branches before full discharge cost is paid.

SMARTINV (Wang et al., S&P 2024, arxiv:2411.09217): THE direct prior art. 'Tier of Thought' (ToT-style) prompting + finetuning. 3.5x more bug-critical invariants and 4x more bug detections than INVCON (Daikon) and VERISMART (CEGIS), 150x less time. Uncovered 119 zero-days on 89,000 contracts. Plumbline Pass A v2.5's mode-split (scalar/relational/temporal/counting) is structurally the same idea, executed as parallel branches.

FLAMES (arxiv:2510.21401, late 2025): state-of-the-art calibration point. Fine-tuned LLMs synthesize Solidity require statements. 96.7% compilability, 44.5% semantic match on 5000-invariant test, blocks 22/108 (20.4%) real exploits. The frontier exploit-mitigation rate is ~20%, NOT 80% — plumbline's 14% CLEAN is much closer to the SOTA frontier than it looks.

Honest budget math: Claude Sonnet 4.5 on OpenRouter = $3/Mtok input, $15/Mtok output (verified openrouter.ai/anthropic/claude-sonnet-4.5). Single Pass A round ~3k in + 1k out = ~$0.024. ToT b=3 depth=2 with critic + halmos terminal = ~7 LLM calls = ~$0.17/contract — ~150 contracts fit in $25. ToT b=5 depth=2 (vanilla) = ~$0.40/contract = ~60 contracts. Sherlock 1259 DRE has ~60-150 in-scope contracts: ToT-b=3 fits comfortably, ToT-b=5 is on the edge.
concrete_examples:
ToT BFS pseudocode (Yao et al. 2023, Algorithm 1):
  S_0 = {x}                                                # root
  for t in 1..T:
    S'_t = { (s, z) : s in S_{t-1}, z in propose(s, k) }   # generate k candidates per state
    V_t  = { (s', value(s')) : s' in S'_t }                # evaluate
    S_t  = top-b by V_t                                     # keep b best
  return best path through S_T
Game-of-24 settings: k=8 proposed thoughts, b=5 kept, depth=3, value=vote-from-3-samples.

Plumbline Pass A v2.5 ToT shape (proposed concretely):
  state = (contract_src, partial_invariant_list)
  propose(state, k=3): extractor LLM yields 3 candidate invariants in current mode
  evaluate(state): two-tier — (a) critic LLM ranks plausibility (cheap), (b) halmos discharge on top-2 (the real signal)
  search: BFS with b=2 (keep top-2 by critic), depth=2 (one expansion round)
  terminal: halmos finds CEX => CLEAN; vacuous discharge => prune; timeout => mark uncertain and keep

AlphaProof tactic-tree analog: at each node, policy net proposes ~16 tactics, MCTS expands by UCB, Lean kernel evaluates leaves. For plumbline at $25: extractor LLM replaces policy net, critic score replaces UCB, halmos replaces Lean kernel. Structure transfers; budget forces b<=3 not b=16.

Best-of-N halmos-picked fallback (the honest MVP):
  candidates = [ extractor(contract, mode=m) for m in [scalar, relational, temporal, counting] ]
  results = [ halmos_discharge(c) for c in candidates ]
  return first c that produced a real CEX, else 'no_finding'
Depth=1 ToT, no critic = 4 LLM calls + 4 halmos runs = ~$0.10/contract = 250 contracts on budget. Likely 70-80% of full ToT recall at 25% cost.

VerMCTS-style partial-invariant pruning: when ToT expands `assume X >= old(X); assume Y unchanged`, run halmos in 'find any model' mode rather than 'prove' mode. If the partial invariant is vacuously true, prune before paying full discharge. Only way to make depth>2 affordable.

SMARTINV Tier-of-Thought prompt structure (from paper): multi-step prompt asks model first to identify state variables, then identify state-transition modes, then propose invariants per mode, then self-rank. Plumbline v2.5's mode-split executes this as parallel branches rather than sequential tiers — which IS a ToT, not a CoT, and is the right adaptation.
plumbline_recommendation: For Day-5 (Friday 2026-06-13), do NOT build full ToT-with-MCTS. Build this concrete shape: Pass A v2.5 = best-of-N + critic-rerank + halmos-terminal, which is structurally ToT with b=N, depth=1, no expansion. Specifically: (1) Run all 4 invariant modes (scalar/relational/temporal/counting) in parallel as the 'propose' step — 4 candidates/contract, ~$0.10. (2) Single critic call scoring all 4 jointly using comparative voting (cheaper than per-candidate value calls; this is the GoT 31%-cost-win pattern applied locally) — ~$0.03. (3) Discharge top-2 with halmos as terminal evaluator — the real signal, not the LLM critic. (4) Only IF a contract produces zero CEX and zero discharge across all 4 modes, expand the top-1 by one critic-feedback round (adaptive depth=2 fallback, bounded at ~10% of contracts). Total: ~$0.15/contract average, ~165 contracts fit in $25, covers Sherlock 1259 DRE comfortably. Skip MCTS — the AlphaProof comparison is structurally honest but budgetarily not. Frame in commits as 'ToT-b=N depth=1 with adaptive depth=2 fallback', cite Yao 2023 + SMARTINV 2024, and measure CLEAN lift vs the 14% baseline. Honest expectation: SMARTINV-class 3-4x lift in bug-critical invariants is achievable; matching FLAMES's 20% exploit-mitigation is the longer-term ceiling, not Day-5.
sources: https://arxiv.org/abs/2305.10601 — Tree of Thoughts: Deliberate Problem Solving with Large Language Models (Yao et al., NeurIPS 2023)
https://arxiv.org/pdf/2305.10601 — ToT full PDF with BFS pseudocode and Game-of-24 cost numbers
https://arxiv.org/abs/2308.09687 — Graph of Thoughts (Besta et al., AAAI 2024)
https://arxiv.org/abs/2203.11171 — Self-Consistency Improves Chain of Thought Reasoning (Wang et al. 2022)
https://www.nature.com/articles/s41586-025-09833-y — Olympiad-level formal mathematical reasoning with RL (AlphaProof, Nature 2025)
https://arxiv.org/abs/2306.15626 — LeanDojo + ReProver (Yang et al., NeurIPS 2023)
https://arxiv.org/pdf/2402.08147 — VerMCTS: Synthesizing Multi-Step Programs using a Verifier, an LLM, and Tree Search
https://arxiv.org/pdf/2501.10069 — Survey on LLM Test-Time Compute via Search (2025)
https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.21401 — FLAMES: Fine-tuning LLMs to Synthesize Invariants for Smart Contract Security

summary: Belief propagation for invariant inference is a REAL technique with a real precedent (Rand & Reps, POPL'07 "Program Verification as Probabilistic Inference") and active 2024-2025 follow-up (SmartInv multimodal, iRank contrastive ranking, PropertyGPT iterative feedback, hybrid LLM+SMT frameworks). But the strict factor-graph / message-passing form (Pearl, Mezard-Parisi-Zecchina Survey Propagation) does NOT fit plumbline's current shape: invariants generated by an LLM do not form a sparse factor graph with locally-defined potentials, and there is no inherent variable/factor-node structure to pass messages across. Where BP genuinely fits SMT-style work is INSIDE a solver (Nelson-Oppen equality propagation, SP for SAT/CSP) — at a lower level than invariant proposal. For plumbline's "magical path-finding in the manifold" intuition, the honest mapping is NOT loopy BP — it's CEGIS (counterexample-guided inductive synthesis) with a belief-update RANKING layer (iRank-style scoring), which captures the probabilistic-belief intuition without claiming convergence theorems plumbline cannot prove by Friday. Survey Propagation has known convergence trouble on short cycles / small instances — plumbline's invariant space is exactly that regime (5-20 candidates per pass, dense semantic overlap). Use CEGAR/CEGIS with weighted candidate ranking; reserve "belief propagation" as a faithful metaphor only, not a hard claim.
key_findings: Rand & Reps (POPL 2007, 'Program Verification as Probabilistic Inference', Microsoft Research) treats invariant inference as Gibbs-sampling-style probabilistic inference on the CFG: each program point holds a belief distribution over candidate constraints (difference constraints in their case study), and updates iterate toward local consistency. This is the closest real precedent to the JH framing — but their belief is over a FIXED predicate vocabulary, not over open-ended LLM-generated invariants. Direct port to plumbline is non-trivial because plumbline's candidate space is generated on demand, not enumerated.

Survey Propagation (Mezard-Parisi-Zecchina, JACM 2007 / Science 2002) is the only message-passing algorithm with empirical near-linear scaling on random k-SAT near the threshold (millions of vars), via 1RSB cavity analysis. Convergence is conjectural even for SAT and KNOWN to fail on short loops / small N. Plumbline operates at small N (5-20 candidates per pass) with semantically dense overlap — exactly the worst regime for SP. Real fit: NO for invariant inference; YES if you reduce a verification subproblem to k-SAT and call an SP-equipped SAT backend (not what plumbline does).

Loopy belief propagation (Ihler et al., JMLR 2005) has formal convergence conditions only for tree-like or weakly-cyclic graphs; on graphs with short cycles, messages oscillate. The Bethe-free-energy fixpoint is a useful frame for analysis but does not give an actionable algorithm for plumbline's ~5-20 candidate space.

iRank (Chakraborty et al., arXiv 2310.09342) — LLM-generated loop invariant ranker via CONTRASTIVE LEARNING on problem-invariant embeddings — moves median rank of correct invariant from 62 → 4 (GPT-3.5) and 9 → 2 (GPT-4). This is the MOST DIRECTLY ACTIONABLE precedent for plumbline. It is NOT belief propagation — it is a learned scoring function — but it IS the 'probabilistic belief over candidates' framing applied concretely. Unidirectional: no LLM feedback loop.

PropertyGPT (NDSS 2025) and SmartInv (IEEE S&P 2024, Wang/Pei/Yang) are the smart-contract analogues. PropertyGPT does iterative compiler-feedback + weighted ranking → top-K. SmartInv uses 'tiers of thought' (easier subproblems guide harder ones) + counterexample-driven bounded model checker fallback. Neither uses belief-propagation primitives; both use CEGIS-like loops with ranking. These are the right reference architectures for plumbline.

Nelson-Oppen theory combination (de Moura/Bjorner 'Model-based Theory Combination') is genuine MESSAGE PASSING — solvers exchange interface equalities — but it lives INSIDE the SMT solver (halmos's backend), one layer below plumbline. Plumbline cannot improve halmos by adding BP at its own (proposer) layer; the BP-style work already happens for free in z3.

Hybrid Framework (arXiv 2508.00419, Aug 2025) confirms the field has converged on LLM-as-proposer + SMT-as-verifier with structured candidate search — not on BP. No 2024-2026 invariant-inference paper of substance uses loopy BP / SP as the top-level orchestrator. That absence is informative: if BP for invariant inference were a real fit, the LLM-era papers would be using it.

HONEST VERDICT for plumbline: BP / SP is metaphor at the invariant-PROPOSAL layer (open-ended candidate space, no fixed factor graph, no locally-defined potentials, N too small for SP's loop-length amortization). BP is real at the SAT/SMT-SOLVER layer (already inside halmos). The JH 'belief over candidate invariants' intuition is best operationalized as CEGIS + a learned/heuristic RANKING signal (iRank-style) over critic-feedback features — which IS the multi-mode v2 proposer + critic gradient framing plumbline already has, just rebadged.
concrete_examples: Loopy BP message update (Pearl form, for reference): m_{i→a}(x_i) = ∏_{b∈N(i)\{a}} m_{b→i}(x_i), m_{a→i}(x_i) = Σ_{x_{N(a)\i}} f_a(x_{N(a)}) ∏_{j∈N(a)\i} m_{j→a}(x_j). Requires: well-defined factors f_a, sparse bipartite structure. Plumbline has neither over LLM-generated invariants.

Survey Propagation cavity-bias update (Mezard-Zecchina): η_{a→i} = ∏_{j∈V(a)\i} (Π^u_{j→a} / (Π^u_{j→a}+Π^s_{j→a}+Π^*_{j→a})) where Π^{u,s,*} are 'cavity biases.' Lives on the SAT clause-variable bipartite factor graph; needs locally-tree-like structure (cycles average O(log N)).

iRank scoring (the realistic plumbline analog): Φ(problem) and Φ(invariant) → embeddings; learned Ψ; score = |cos(Ψ(Φ(p)), Φ(inv))|; trained with contrastive MSE loss vs {0,1} labels. Concrete result: median rank of correct invariant 62→4 (GPT-3.5), 9→2 (GPT-4).

CEGIS skeleton for plumbline (what we'd actually build): for round t in 1..T: candidates_t = Proposer(context, criticfeedback_{<t}); for c in candidates_t: verdict, cex = halmos(c); update belief(c) ← f(verdict, cex, ranking_features); if any verdict == PROVED: return c; criticfeedback_t = top-k(belief)+cex_summary. The 'belief' here is a SCORE, not a marginalized BP posterior — and that's honest.

Probabilistic CEGAR (Hermanns/Wachter/Zhang 2008, 0807.1173) — the legitimate 'probabilistic CEGAR' variant — operates on Markov Decision Processes, not on invariant candidate spaces. It refines an MDP abstraction using probability-bounded counterexamples. Plumbline's smart-contract problem is not naturally an MDP, so this branch is not a fit.
plumbline_recommendation: SKIP belief propagation / Survey Propagation as a core algorithm — they are metaphors at plumbline's layer and not implementable by Friday in a way that beats CEGIS. Within the Day-5 cutoff (Fri 2026-06-13) for structural_proposer fire-and-measure on Sherlock 1259 DRE, do this instead: (1) Keep the Pass A v2 multi-mode proposer. (2) Add a lightweight CEGIS LOOP — proposer → halmos → on COUNTEREXAMPLE feed the cex (concrete state, txn ordering, values) BACK into the proposer prompt for round 2; on TIMEOUT feed 'simplify or strengthen' hint; on PASS-VACUOUS feed the reachability gap. Cap at 3 rounds (budget: ~$0.50 per finding × 20 candidates × 3 rounds ≈ $30, just under the $25 OpenRouter cap if you skip round-3 for failures of round-1 and round-2). (3) Add a SCALAR 'belief score' per candidate that aggregates: (a) halmos verdict (PROVED=1, COUNTEREX_with_cex=0.6, TIMEOUT=0.3, PASS_VACUOUS=0.1), (b) syntactic novelty vs prior candidates, (c) mode-tag (relational > scalar for DRE-class bugs). Use the score to pick which candidate to refine, NOT to claim BP convergence. Frame this in commits as 'CEGIS + ranked-belief candidates' — it captures JH's manifold-path-finding intuition honestly without overclaiming a Mezard-Parisi-Zecchina result you cannot prove. If after Sherlock 1259 you want to revisit BP seriously, the right entry is to apply iRank's contrastive-learning ranker to plumbline's growing corpus of (candidate, verdict) pairs — that is the future-work BP-adjacent move, not Friday's move.
sources: https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/1255443.1255445 (A new look at survey propagation and its generalizations, JACM 2007)
https://jmlr.org/papers/v6/ihler05a.html (Ihler et al., Loopy BP Convergence, JMLR 2005)
https://arxiv.org/pdf/2508.00419 (Hybrid LLM+SMT framework for loop invariants, 2025)

summary: Plumbline's "magical path-finding" should land as CEGAR-lite: a critic-refute → re-extract loop with K=3 iterations, composing zero-touch on top of v2 multi-mode Pass A. The literature is unambiguous that this is the highest-yield, lowest-cost addition for a Day-5 cutoff. Loopy's Houdini-over-15-completions gave +30.7% (Kamath et al., 2023). LLM+BMC+counterexample loops on loop-invariant benchmarks lift single-shot 78% → 98.2% in ≤8 repair iterations (NAGAI/SciTeP 2024; arXiv 2511.06552). The reasoning-LLM+SMT loop (arXiv 2508.00419) hits 100% in 1.0–1.37 avg iterations on 133 benchmarks. Self-consistency without a feedback signal is the WORST option for plumbline: HotpotQA shows +0.4% for 20× cost, and MATH-500 actually degrades past N=15 (arXiv 2511.00751). ToT and MCTS are overkill at this budget — they require a reward signal stronger than "critic-refute Y/N", which plumbline doesn't have. Halmos-on-critical-path is the right call but Halmos itself is not the critic in Pass A (no Solidity body yet); the existing adversarial-critic LLM IS the cheap oracle. The 43/50 v1 refusals are dominated by "vacuous-when-bug-fires" — exactly the failure mode a critic-feedback loop maximally fixes, because the critic's refutation reason IS the gradient signal back to the extractor.
key_findings: Loopy (Microsoft, Kamath et al. 2023, arXiv 2311.07948) — N=15 LLM completions + Houdini pruning lifts success +30.7% (293→383 benchmarks). This is the precedent: many candidates + cheap post-filter beats one careful candidate. Plumbline already does N=1; even N=3 with critic-prune is likely the single largest CLEAN-rate lever.

Counterexample-refinement loops (arXiv 2508.00419 LLM+SMT, ConVer arXiv 2605.27051 — 'CEGAR runs up to 5 iterations before escalating', LLM-BMC ASE24) converge in <2 rounds on average and lift single-shot near-100%. The critical insight: the SMT counterexample is fed back AS TEXT into the next prompt. Plumbline's adversarial critic already produces text refutations — this is a drop-in replacement for SMT counterexamples at zero infrastructure cost.

Self-consistency / pure best-of-N WITHOUT a verifier is the wrong move for plumbline at this budget. arXiv 2511.00751 (2025): HotpotQA +0.4% for 20× cost, MATH-500 DEGRADES past N=15. Lesson: spend the budget on critic-conditioned re-extraction (1-bit gradient), not on uninformed parallel sampling.

ToT and MCTS over (mode, refinement-depth) are out-of-scope for Day-5. arXiv 2502.05310 'Oracular Programming' and ToT survey work shows ToT only beats CoT+self-consistency when a per-node value function exists. Plumbline has only a binary refuted/clean signal — insufficient signal density for tree search. Falling back to MCTS pre-Day-5 is architecture astronautics.

The v1 14% CLEAN failure mode is dominated by 'invariant holds vacuously when bug fires' (visible in both validation.md rows shown — zksync conditional-enforce, insure user_balance_only_decreases). This is EXACTLY the failure mode that re-extract-with-critic-reason fixes maximally, because the critic verbalizes the vacuity in plain English. The expected-value math is: if critic is 70% accurate at refute and re-extraction conditioned on the refutation reason converts 50% of refutes, then CLEAN goes 14% → 14% + 0.86*0.7*0.5 ≈ 44%.

Multi-mode v2 + CEGAR-lite COMPOSE multiplicatively, not additively. v2's contribution is enlarging the hypothesis space (scalar/relational/temporal/counting/null modes). CEGAR-lite's contribution is search within that space conditioned on critic signal. Both attack orthogonal failure modes (v1's narrowness AND v1's single-shot rigidity). H13 should test this product structure, not each in isolation.

Halmos as terminal oracle is NOT yet wired into Pass A annotation (it operates on Solidity, Pass A operates on natural-language findings). The correct Day-5 design keeps the LLM critic as the loop oracle for Pass A and reserves Halmos for the downstream Solidity-rendering pass. Trying to put Halmos in the Pass A loop in 3 days = scope explosion.

API cost for K=3 critic-refute loop on N=50 corpus with Sonnet 4.5 ≈ $4-6, well within $25 budget. Each finding: 1 extract (in:1500/out:600) + up-to-2 re-extracts (in:2500/out:600) + 3 critics (in:1500/out:200). At Sonnet 4.5 pricing ~$3/M in, $15/M out, per-finding ≈ $0.08, total ≈ $4.00 for N=50. Leaves $19 headroom for prompt iteration.
concrete_examples:
# re_extract prompt template (new prompts/sol_invariant_reextract.md)
# Reuses sol_invariant_propose.md and APPENDS:

REEXTRACT_SUFFIX = '''

## PREVIOUS ATTEMPT (REFUTED by adversarial critic)

You previously extracted this invariant:
```json
{prev_inv_json}
```

The adversarial critic refuted it with this reason:
> {critic_reason}

The most common failure mode is: the invariant HOLDS VACUOUSLY when the bug fires. Re-extract a STRONGER invariant that would be VIOLATED by the bug. Consider switching representation_mode (scalar/relational/temporal/counting) if the current mode cannot express the violation. Return the same JSON schema.
'''

# Kill criterion (executable check before Day-5):
# Run on a HELDOUT 20-finding subset Friday morning.
# Decision tree:
#   if CLEAN(C2) < CLEAN(C1) + 4 percentage points: REVERT to K=1. CEGAR-lite is dead.
#   if API spend > $12 on the 20-finding probe: REVERT (extrapolates to >$30 on N=50).
#   if mean rounds-to-clean > 2.3: WARNING — convergence is slow; ship K=2 not K=3.
#   else: ship K=3 with N=50 full run.
#
# This is a strict, pre-committed kill. No moving the goalposts after the run.
plumbline_recommendation: SHIP CEGAR-lite K=3 (initial + 2 critic-conditioned re-extracts) on top of v2 multi-mode. Day-5 plan: Day 1 morning — write prompts/sol_invariant_reextract.md (appends prev_inv + critic_reason to existing sol_invariant_propose.md), add re_extract_with_critic() and the K=3 loop in annotate_corpus_invariants.py (~50 LOC, the existing extract_and_validate is already 80% of the structure). Day 1 afternoon — run on N=20 heldout. Apply kill criterion. Day 2 morning — if alive, run N=50 + log rounds-to-clean histogram. Day 2 afternoon — write the H13 pre-registration into arXiv Section 7. Do NOT add ToT, MCTS, or self-consistency-over-N. Do NOT put Halmos in the Pass A loop. Do NOT touch the schema. The critic already produces the gradient — wire it back, then measure.
sources: https://arxiv.org/pdf/2311.07948 — Kamath et al., Finding Inductive Loop Invariants using LLMs (Loopy, Houdini +30.7% at N=15)
https://arxiv.org/html/2508.00419v1 — Hybrid LLM+SMT reasoning loop, 1.0-1.37 avg iterations on 133 benchmarks, 100% coverage
https://chentaolue.github.io/pub-papers/ASE24.pdf — LLM Meets Bounded Model Checking (Neuro-symbolic Loop Invariant Inference, ASE 2024)
https://arxiv.org/abs/2511.06552 — LLM For Loop Invariant Generation and Fixing: How Far Are We? (78% gen, 16% repair w/o feedback)
https://arxiv.org/html/2511.00751 — Self-Consistency Diminishing Returns (HotpotQA +0.4% at N=20; MATH-500 degrades past N=15)
https://arxiv.org/pdf/2305.11860 — Adaptive-Consistency, 3.3x sample reduction with 0.04% accuracy loss (early-stop signal for K-tuning)
https://arxiv.org/pdf/2510.21401 — FLAMES: Fine-tuning LLMs to Synthesize Invariants for Smart Contract Security
https://arxiv.org/pdf/2411.09217 — SmartInv: Multimodal Learning for Smart Contract Invariant Inference (mode-multiplexing precedent)
https://arxiv.org/html/2605.27051 — ConVer (CEGAR up to 5 iterations before escalating, LLM+contracts for Solidity)
https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/3806652 — Guiding LLM-Based Loop Invariant Synthesis via Feedback on Local Reasoning Errors (Frama-C, TOPLAS 2025)
https://arxiv.org/pdf/2502.05310 — Oracular Programming: Modular Foundation for LLM-Enabled Software (ToT requires value function — plumbline doesn't have one)
